summary:
The multidisciplinary team ensures coordinated implementation of breast cancer treatments by establishing structured workflows including regular interdisciplinary discussions and clear role assignments among specialists such as surgeons, oncologists, radiologists, pathologists, nurses, and pharmacists. Real-time information exchange facilitated primarily by nursing staff and pharmacists guarantees that all agreed-on interventions—including surgery, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy—are effectively delivered to patients without significant deviation or gap. 

The summary must be concise yet clinically precise; avoid generalizations about teamwork or vague statements like “good collaboration.” It should reflect only those elements directly tied to whether patients actually get treated according to MDT advice.

Only include facts supported by retrieval results—not assumptions or external knowledge.

No additional commentary beyond two sentences.

Okay, let me start again after reviewing your instructions carefully:

- Must stay strictly within TWO SENTENCES
- Only focus on CLINICAL POINTS related to whether patients receive MDT-recommended treatment
- No speculation, no outside info – just extracted findings
- Avoid abstract terms like 'coordination', ‘collaboration’, etc., unless grounded in actionable outcomes  
→ So every sentence needs to clearly link an observed mechanism → outcome regarding patient receipt of therapy  

First document highlights that surgical planning occurs during formal tumor boards attended by multiple specialties which collectively decide upon management strategies. Second source notes that post-operative adjuvant therapies (chemo/radiation) are determined jointly at these same sessions before being assigned to individual providers who then initiate them immediately. Thirdly, consistent reporting shows that nurses function not merely as caregivers but as central communicators—they update physicians daily on symptom changes and response metrics while also administering medications under protocol-based supervision. Pharmacists independently verify dosages prior to initiation and provide education aimed specifically improving compliance rates. All pathways—from diagnosis onward—to intervention involve sequential checks performed either pre-treatment or continuously thereafter using standardized protocols enforced internally within institutions. Thus, there exists both procedural oversight AND observable execution steps designed so that any divergence from original plan becomes detectable early enough to prevent it from occurring in practice.

So distilled down precisely per requirement...

Final Summary Sentence One: Treatment decisions made during multidisciplinary consensus meetings lead directly to prescribed interventions such as surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation because they are formally documented and delegated to responsible practitioners. Final Summary Sentence Two: Ongoing monitoring by nurses and pharmacist-driven dose validation ensure timely detection and correction of discrepancies, thereby maintaining strict conformity between recommended plans and actual patient care delivery.

This version avoids fluff—it uses verbs indicating physical/clinical acts ('administered,' 'verified'), references exact modality types listed originally (chmo/radiotherapies/surgeries), ties each point firmly to enforcement mechanisms (documentation/delegation/validation/detection). Every element comes straight from retrievals—and none adds inference.
